text: Extra-levator abdominoperoneal resection should be considered for patients with low rectal tumour where distal margin clearance is not possible or if the tumour is invading the anal sphincters. Sometimes it should also be considered for patients with very poor sphincter control who may not tolerate a coloanal anastomosis.
The patient is positioned in the Lloyd Davis position, with legs elevated and spread apart, to provide optimal access to the pelvic region for the abdominal part of the operation.
Before starting the procedure, an examination under anaesthesia should be performed by the surgeon to reassess and confirm the level of tumour and sphincter involvement.
The surgeon makes a low midline incision on the patient's abdomen, providing access to the pelvic region for the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon performs a laparotomy to explore the abdomen, visually inspecting the liver, peritoneal cavity, and pelvis for signs of metastatic disease.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon by incising along the line of Toldt, separating the colon from the lateral peritoneal attachments.
The surgeon identifies the ureters at the pelvic brim and ensures their protection during the procedure.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum and mobilizes the rectum down the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, reaching the level of the coccyx posteriorly.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field to preserve the pelvic nerves during the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon develops the Denonvilliers fascia anteriorly, extending the dissection to just below the seminal vesicles in men and the vagina in women.
The surgeon ligates and divides the inferior mesenteric vessels, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the sigmoid colon, completing the separation of this section from the rest of the colon.
A medium swab is sutured to the proximal end of the rectal specimen and placed in the presacral space anterior to the coccyx, preparing the area for the perineal phase of the operation.
The surgeon forms a left iliac fossa colostomy at the premarked site, maturing it after confirming that the proximal colon is well-vascularized and adequately mobilized.
The surgeon completes the abdominal part of the operation by suturing the incision closed, ensuring the abdominal cavity is securely sealed.
Dressings are applied to the surgical wounds, and a gelonet and gauze are placed over the stoma to protect it while the patient is in a prone position.
The surgical team carefully turns the patient and positions them in the prone jackknife position, with the legs slightly abducted, preparing for the next phase of the operation.
Pressure areas must be protected.
The surgeon tapes the buttocks apart to enhance exposure of the surgical site.
The surgeon closes the anus using a double purse string suture technique, ensuring the area is sealed to minimize the risk of contamination.
In the prone jackknife position, the surgeon palpates the coccyx and sacrococcygeal joint, marking the coccyx for further surgical steps.
The surgeon makes a teardrop-shaped skin incision around the anus, outside the sphincter complex, extending the incision just beyond the sacrococcygeal joint.

The surgeon performs dissection around the external anal sphincter, maintaining proximity to the sphincter and avoiding the fat envelope of the ischiorectal fossa, unless the external sphincter is involved.
A lone star retractor is positioned to enhance exposure of the surgical field, providing a clearer view of the anatomical structures involved in the rectal cancer procedure.

The surgeon initiates the plane of resection along the external anal sphincter, extending laterally towards the levator ani muscle. The dissection continues to the insertion of the levator muscles into the obturator muscle on the pelvic side wall.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly, focusing on the coccyx and sacrococcygeal joint, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon evaluates whether to disarticulate the coccyx at the sacrococcygeal joint or leave it intact, focusing on the anatomical area around the joint.
The surgeon divides the fascia to enter the peritoneal cavity, revealing the medium swab placed in the presacral space during the abdominal portion of the operation.
The surgeon divides the levator muscles on both sides, creating a large opening to facilitate the retrieval of the medium swab from the presacral space and the delivery of the specimen.
The specimen shows the levator muscles encircling the lower rectum, forming a cylindrical shape without any tissue wastage.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, separating it from the prostate or vaginal vault under direct vision, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures involved.
Care must be taken during this part of the dissection to avoid injury to the urethra.
The surgeon uses argon gas as an adjunct to achieve haemostasis, visibly reducing bleeding in the surgical field.
The video shows the pelvic floor defect remaining after the extra levator abdominoperoneal resection, with visible absence of the rectum and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon reconstructs the pelvic floor using a 10 cm by 10 cm Biodesign biological mesh from Cook Medical, positioning the mesh to cover the defect left after the resection.
The surgeon rehydrates a 10 cm by 10 cm Biodesign biological mesh in 0.9% saline solution for less than one minute, preparing it for placement in the pelvic floor reconstruction.
The surgeon secures the biological mesh to the cut edge of the pelvic floor using interrupted sutures with Proline or PDS, ensuring the mesh is firmly attached to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon inserts multiple sutures around the pelvic floor defect without tying them, facilitating the accurate placement of the Biodesign biological mesh.
After securing the mesh to the pelvic floor, the surgeon places a suction drain over the mesh to facilitate drainage.
The surgeon releases the tape from the buttocks and approximates the fat in layers using interrupted absorbable sutures, visibly minimizing the dead space in the surgical area.
The surgeon places mattress silk sutures to approximate the skin, ensuring closure of the surgical site.
The patient is then transferred to a post-operative bed.
The surgeon must review the stoma to ensure that it is still viable before the patient is woken up by the anaesthetist.
We recommend that the patient is nursed on their side for the first forty-eight hours and encouraged to mobilise without sitting on their perineum for extended periods within the first week.
